questions.text: I believe the most admirable people are those who give the most of themselves.
I feel frustrated when leadership is unclear or absent.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most clearly reveals what I truly care about.
I rarely cry, not because I suppress emotions but because they simply don't reach that intensity.
I feel that willpower is like a muscle that grows stronger with regular use.
I find myself naturally taking on extra work so that others don't have to.
I see myself as someone who helps things happen.
I think that the discipline of emotional detachment during crises is a capacity that can and should be trained.
I can read the atmosphere of a meeting within seconds of walking in.
I feel that building my own career and reputation is a central life priority.
I regularly take initiative in situations where others hesitate.
I experience love as an almost overwhelming force rather than a steady warmth.
I am usually more aware of the logical flow of a conversation than its emotional currents.
I start tasks with whatever is at hand and improvise with available resources.
I often catch the brief moment of hesitation before someone agrees to something.
I find that I'm easily stirred by stories, films, or events that others take in stride.
I feel that the effort of maintaining order is always worth the payoff in clarity.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that gives my ethical principles their substance and their force.
